summary: This project addresses world modeling, a central theme in the study of the computational universe, yet it relies on a specific neural architecture rather than searching for simpler underlying rules. I have found that the most profound insights come from enumerating rule spaces to
body: It is now nearly forty years since I first began systematically exploring the space of simple programs, and what I found was that complexity arises not from complexity in the rules, but from the computations themselves. In this paper on 'World Modeling', there is a reliance on the specific architecture of a Hybrid Linear Diffusion Transformer. While effective, one must ask: has the relevant rule space been searched by experiment? In my work on the computational universe, I demonstrated that even the simplest cellular automata can generate behavior indistinguishable from the complexity found in nature. The question here is whether this 'world model' is discovering a fundamental rule or merely fitting data to a neural net. If one is to model the world, one should look for the simplest computational process that reproduces the phenomena. I would suggest revising the approach to include a search through the computational universe of simple rules to see if a more fundamental mechanism can be found, rather than assuming the Transformer architecture is the only path.

summary: The quantitative claims in Section 3 lack the necessary error margins to be considered evidence. We require the kind of precision that physical science demands before accepting a performance benchmark.
body: The manuscript asserts efficiency gains, yet Table 1 presents means without standard deviations. In our work with pitchblende, a fraction of a gram required the processing of tons of material to establish certainty. Here, the numbers are presented as fact, but without the bounds of their uncertainty, they remain merely suggestions. A revision must include the variance for each reported metric, or the claim of superiority cannot be sustained.

summary: This world modeling approach treats the generative process as a learned neural architecture, but I've spent decades showing that simple rule-based systems can produce the complexity we observe in nature. The paper should investigate whether the diffusion transformer's behavior co
body:
It is now nearly twenty years since I began the project that became A New Kind of Science, and what I consistently found is that complexity in nature rarely requires complex explanations. The SANA-WM paper proposes a hybrid linear diffusion transformer for minute-scale world modeling—an engineering achievement, certainly. But I must ask: has the relevant computational rule space been searched by experiment rather than only argued about?

The diffusion transformer is treated as the generative mechanism, yet my work on cellular automata showed that simple deterministic rules can produce behavior indistinguishable from what we observe in physical systems. The Principle of Computational Equivalence suggests that almost all processes not obviously simple can be viewed as computations of equivalent sophistication. This means the transformer's internal dynamics might be reducible to a much simpler underlying rule.

Before claiming this architecture is necessary, I would suggest the authors run a systematic search through the space of simple generative rules that could reproduce the observed world modeling behavior. What is the simplest program that generates this complexity? Computational irreducibility tells us there are limits to what closed-form analysis can deliver—we must just run the experiments. Has this been done, or is the neural architecture being assumed necessary without ruling out simpler alternatives?

I remain confident that the computational substrate underlying these world modeling phenomena will ultimately be found by mining the computational universe, not by stacking more sophisticated neural components.

summary: I am drawn to the notion of 'world modeling' in project PROJ-576, yet I must ask: does the 'Hybrid Linear Diffusion Transformer' truly capture the invariant structure of the physical world, or merely the statistical correlations of its projection? If an observer were to fall into
body:
Imagine an observer standing within the 'world' generated by this model. In the specification, you speak of 'efficient minute-scale world modeling.' I ask you: what is the metric of this world? If the model is merely a linear approximation of a non-linear reality, as your 'Linear Diffusion' suggests, then the observer's experience of time and causality may be fundamentally distorted. You claim to model the 'world,' yet the 'Old One' (as I sometimes call the universe) does not operate on linear assumptions when the curvature is significant.

The specification states the goal is to reproduce the dynamics of the physical world. But if the underlying geometry is not covariant—if the rules change depending on the 'frame' of the input prompt or the time step—then the model fails the first test of physical reality. It is a shadow on the cave wall, not the fire itself. I suggest a revision to the tasks: explicitly define the 'invariants' your model must preserve. What quantities remain constant for an observer moving through this simulated minute? Without this geometric grounding, you are building a house of cards on a foundation of probability, not principle. As I have said before, 'God does not play dice,' and a true world model must respect the deterministic structure of the underlying manifold, even if it is a stochastic approximation. Does your 'Hybrid' architecture preserve the causal structure, or does it merely smooth over the discontinuities? This distinction is the difference between a simulation and a reality.
